one_line_takeaway: MAGMA reframes malware detection as retrieval-grounded, multi-agent probabilistic evidence evaluation, using the Shannon entropy of a 5-agent stochastic ensemble's votes (ECS) as a calibrated fail-safe rejection signal that lets the system abstain (98.4% F1 when it does decide) instead of confidently misclassifying structurally novel or evasive binaries.
key_finding: On the held-out test set (Table 6), MAGMA (N=5) reaches Acc/Prec/Rec/F1 = 98.4% each, versus ResNeXt+ at 97.1% F1 (+1.3pp) and MalConv at 94.2% F1 (+4.2pp), at ~42s per binary versus ~0.8s (ResNeXt+) and ~0.3s (MalConv). The ablation (Table 8) attributes this gain componentwise: k-NN-embedding-only reaches 95.8% F1; adding a single LLM agent (N=1) adds +0.6pp; N=3 adds +1.8pp; N=5 adds +2.6pp total over the embedding-only baseline; LLM zero-shot without retrieval collapses to 79.6% F1, showing retrieval grounding -- not the LLM alone -- is necessary. FES/ECS act as calibration signals (Table 9, N=5): True Positives show FES~0.87/ECS~0.12, True Negatives FES~0.14/ECS~0.14, while False Positives (FES~0.68/ECS~0.82) and False Negatives (FES~0.32/ECS~0.86) cluster near the decision boundary with elevated entropy, i.e., ECS correlates with actual error likelihood. At N=2 this separation collapses (stated but not tabulated), showing the diagnostic value requires ensemble diversity, not mere redundancy. The tau_stable trade-off (Table 5) is explicit: permissive tau>=0.95 gives FPR 3.2%/FNR 4.1%/rejection 0.5%; the chosen default tau=0.80 gives FPR 1.6%/FNR 1.6%/rejection 4.2%; zero-trust tau<=0.60 gives FPR 0.3%/FNR 0.5%/rejection 14.8%. Latency (Table 11): LLM ensemble inference dominates at ~74% of the ~42s total (31.5s of 5-DCF ensemble calls), versus ~4.5s for k-NN-only mode (9.3x faster, Table 12) -- the paper positions MAGMA as a Tier-2 cloud verification layer for the 'gray zone' (~0.1% of daily traffic) rather than a high-throughput endpoint AV replacement.
core_contribution: The paper presents MAGMA (Multi-Agent Generative Malware Analysis), a malware detection pipeline that replaces monolithic point-estimate classification with a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture coupled to a stochastic multi-agent ensemble. MAGMA lifts a binary into two parallel representations -- assembly-level embeddings (e_asm, via a BERT-based encoder fine-tuned per ISA) and decompiled pseudo-code embeddings (e_dec, via Qwen3-Embedding) -- concatenates them into a per-function query vector, and retrieves k-nearest Decision-Critical Functions (DCFs) from a curated Vector Knowledge Base. N=5 instances of the same LLM (Gemma3 27b, run locally via Ollama) then independently vote Malicious/Benign over the retrieved evidence under temperature T=0.7 sampling. From these votes the paper derives two novel metrics computed by the paper's own pipeline (not borrowed from prior work): Function Evidence Strength (FES), the mean fraction of agents voting Malicious weighted by retrieval-set similarity W(R); and Evidence Conflict Score (ECS), the Shannon entropy of the ensemble's Bernoulli vote distribution. A tri-state decision rule (Malicious / Benign / Uncertain) uses FES thresholds (delta_high, delta_low) gated by an ECS stability threshold (tau_stable) to trigger a fail-safe rejection ('Uncertain') that routes ambiguous or adversarially perturbed samples to human review instead of forcing a binary verdict.
threat_model: Formalized in Section 2.2 as the paper's own scoping (Table 1), not cited from elsewhere. Adversary: a strategic Probabilistic Polynomial-Time (PPT) attacker operating a problem-space evasion engine E. Knowledge level: Gray-Box -- the adversary knows MAGMA uses an LLM-based RAG pipeline over dual-stream features but has no access to the Vector Knowledge Base, agent weights, or prompts. Oracle access: hard-label and confidence-score observation via iterative probing O(x). Perturbation space: instruction substitution, dead-code insertion, and basic-block transposition, bounded by a perturbation budget (delta <= epsilon). Adversarial objective is decomposed into three simultaneous constraints the attacker must satisfy: (1) semantic equivalence Func(x')=Func(x); (2) suppress FES(x') below delta_low; (3) keep ECS(x') below tau_stable to avoid triggering the entropy-based rejection ('epistemic concealment'). Explicitly out of scope: dynamic/sandbox evasion ('red-pill' checks), heavy obfuscation such as full VMProtect-style virtualization, and poisoning of the Vector Knowledge Base (treated as a trusted TCB component).
limitations: Stated by the authors in Section 5 (Discussion & Limitations, as extracted): (1) Computational overhead -- O(N*(R+I)) versus MalConv's O(L) single-pass, latency measured in seconds not milliseconds, explicitly not intended to replace high-throughput endpoint AV engines. (2) No claimed resilience against heavy obfuscation (e.g., full VMProtect-style virtualization) or 'red-pill' environmental/sandbox-detection checks -- these are declared out of scope by the threat model itself, not merely a discovered weakness. (3) Remaining susceptibility to 'Opaque Predicate Explosion,' where an evasion engine injects massive junk control flow to dilute k-NN retrieval quality (discussion cuts off mid-explanation at the extracted page limit, so the paper's proposed mitigation for this specific attack, if any, was not captured). (4) Empirically observed error modes (Section 4.4, Table 7): false positives concentrate on legitimate crypto/compression code (AES/zlib/LZ4), raw-socket network diagnostic tools resembling C2 beaconing, and JIT/code-gen applications producing shellcode-like patterns; false negatives concentrate on minimal single-function downloaders (too low cyclomatic complexity to pass the DCF filter meaningfully) and fileless/memory-only stubs (too few extractable DCFs for a robust embedding). (5) The Vector Knowledge Base is explicitly treated as a trusted TCB and its poisoning is declared out of scope, meaning the system's security guarantees assume KB integrity rather than defending it.

datasets_used: Wild-Augmented Benchmark (D_wild): 40,814 binaries total (19.2 GB), combining unpacked SOREL-20M samples with a 2021-2024 long-tail augmentation from VirusShare

D_mal: 16,694 malware samples (11 GB) across 17 families (e.g., Virlock 1,671; Neshta 1,431; Ramnit 1,345; Floxif 1,293; Gosys 1,280; Comet 1,035; VJadtre 953; Doboc 890; Sality 713; ~3,000 across 7 other families)
D_ben: 24,120 benign samples (8.2 GB) from clean Windows 10/11 installs and verified SourceForge/GitHub release binaries
Chronological split by first-seen timestamp: Knowledge Base 70%, Validation 10%, held-out Test set 20%, disjoint with no binary overlap
quantitative_results: Ablation - k-NN + LLM N=5 (=full MAGMA): 98.4% F1
FES/ECS by outcome (N=5): TP FES=0.87/ECS=0.12; TN FES=0.14/ECS=0.14; FP FES=0.68/ECS=0.82; FN FES=0.32/ECS=0.86
tau_stable sweep: tau>=0.95 -> FPR 3.2%, FNR 4.1%, HITL rejection 0.5%; tau=0.80 (default) -> FPR 1.6%, FNR 1.6%, rejection 4.2%; tau<=0.60 -> FPR 0.3%, FNR 0.5%, rejection 14.8%
Retrieval similarity: mean s=0.847 +/- 0.042 across test queries, all above sigma_min=0.70
k-NN voting confidence margin above 0.50 boundary: TP 0.584 (+0.084), TN 0.612 (+0.112), FP 0.506 (+0.006), FN 0.509 (+0.009)
Latency breakdown (N=5, M=5 DCFs): headless Ghidra 1.8s, dual-stream lifting 3.5s, embedding 1.2s, k-NN retrieval 0.03s, LLM ensemble 31.5s (~74% of ~42s total)
Throughput: k-NN-only ~4.5s/binary (9.3x faster) vs full pipeline ~42s/binary
baselines_compared: ResNeXt+ (PE binary rendered as grayscale image, ResNeXt-50 CNN): F1=97.1%, ~0.8s/binary, FNR=2.4%
MalConv (end-to-end raw-byte CNN with temporal max-pooling): F1=94.2%, ~0.3s/binary, elevated FPR=5.6%
k-NN Embedding only (MAGMA's own dual-stream embeddings, similarity-weighted label voting, no LLM): F1=95.8%
LLM Zero-Shot (same agent ensemble but RAG/retrieval disabled): F1=79.6%
